author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text: We'd been using an old vacuum like this for decades. An extra long hose let us connect it to routers and sanders etc, and when we are done we disconnect the machine and connect a circular brush to the hose to pick up any dust that escaped. We finally ran rain-water pipes around the workshop so we had convenient connection points for the hoses. It saved us a fortune instead of buying a purpose built extractor system.

author: @kiddiescripterkiller
text:
I looked in to gasifiers and isn't the part where you want to use the cyclone, normally clears the gas from the tars and other stuff?  It would be hard to clean out the cyclone... Ones I have seen use a super course steel pads stuffed in a pipe that drains to a glass jar... 

And using vacs for woodworking... Make a Thein baffle... Holds more sawdust and chips, less cleaning out and you have more suction from the vacuum throughout the whole use until the baffle gets full... My shop vac, I never have to clean the filter and I'm using it a lot on a CNC, cutting grooves in MDF which is a lot of superfine dust... Before I built the baffle, I would get half way through a board and have to clean the shop vac filter... It would cover the filter like vacuuming up sheetrock dust.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Do those kind of motors have centrifugal switches to change the way field coils and winding coils are wired, once it upto speed? Changes the torque v rpm curves etc.
NB Such switches are often the cause of failure... And why "percussive maintance" can occasionally help.

author: @totherarf
text:
I have a shop vac that I adapted to run through a cyclone dust separator ........ made from an old bucket!

I have to be honest .... I got the cyclone section from the web for £7 but I simply made a flat plate to screw it onto. This fits snugly into the top of a plastic bucket. The vacuum was a tad strong so I ended up screwing a couple of pieces of wood to the inside of the bucket to stop it bellowing in and to keep the top plate flat (the vacuum holds it tight and sucks any small air leaks shut)! No more cleaning out those faffy little filters!

This gives me a whole bucket full of sawdust and chippings that is easy to empty out and the filter in the vac needs emptying once a season (if I feel like it) ;0) I find in my "shop" it works best to have this stationary with a hose that will stretch to the tools .... but if you have a bigger shop and a spare pallet you cn easily construct a "cart" to drag it round!

There is a problem though ..... watching those chips spiraling down the clear cyclone section can be a bit addictive!

author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
ah I have a 1 foot square ultra small particle air filter I had planned for slowing air flow so dust would be trapped from my CNC unit since I have had experience with so much MDF dust, even nearly destroyed a 30 year old dremel that I was using for the head originally, (which sadly they designed to pull air in from the bottom and blow the air out the back near the AC cord, ie sucks up the dust and pulls it through all the bearing areas and such lol)

luckily I saved that thing by pouring 3-in-1 machine oil on top of the bearings while rotating the shaft so it would soak in and flush out most of the grime, :) and yes it still works though once of these days I need to make wedges to push the bearings off and put some new ones on that tool as it is quite the useful tool. 

anyway on the dust collection I have seen it seems the trick is to use high suction over small tubing to grab it but dump this through a large container before it exits out the small hole on the other side so it moves more slowly across the larger diameter chamber where it gets stuck no long having the energy to keep it aloft, and they are built to disassemble and dump what has collected in there each day with the exception of a really large place that made 1000s of MDF based structures for stores and theirs it seemed was weight based so when it got enough weight of dust in the container it caused the bottom to drop down emptying and when empty and lighter the bottom was pushed or pulled back up with a spring sealing it up so t he air flow finished sucking it up to the bottom keeping it closed while continuing to pull that dust from their industrial routers LOL

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Clogged filters ! ... Even cyclones. usual to exhaust the sucked air over motor for cooling. So they have a filter between "usual dust collection" and motor. 

Reverse  flow of air across filter can often restore suction. Probably against H&S/take some care....compressed air will usually shift the clogging.  Be prepared for masses of fine dust!

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Might "Programable unijunction transistor and SCR motor pwm circuit" help with such? ... Pulses a DC voltage off regularly etc.

NB rather than usual "resistor programming" it is far easier to set timebase via fixed current regulators. Fixed current though variable resistor for trigger voltage. 
Use  current reg of slightly smaller amps to set charging rate. capacitor has constant dV/dT, easing timing calcs. and "put conductive latch on" avoided.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Respectfully, that may sound like convenience and clever,  but maybe instead it is more restrictive, running only when you pull the saw trigger. 

Having the vacuum/dust collector  on it's own switch is probably safer and cheaper than digging into the saw's trigger while concurently leaves you free to hook up your dust collector to a sander, planer, or another tool, flipping the dust collector's switch when you need it on or off.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
@kreynolds1123
replies.text: @stevecummins324 in theory that might work, although the stator shouldn't have much if any magnetic leakage.  many tuns of wire around the hot mains wire can be used to detect  current in the mains. coiling the hot wire is sure to be smaller than coilling the motor.
